coredata.dc:title: High-performance supercapacitors using synergistic hierarchical Ni-doped copper compounds/activated carbon composites with MXenes and carbon dots as simultaneous performance enhancers
coredata.dc:creator: Jorn-am, Thanapat
Pholauyphon, Wasinee
Supchocksoonthorn, Preeyanuch
Sirisit, Natee
Chanthad, Chalathorn
Manyam, Jedsada
Liang, Xiao
Song, Shufeng
Paoprasert, Peerasak
coredata.dc:description: For the fabrication of high-performance supercapacitors, materials with nano-array architecture and the use of multiple-component electrodes are emerging as the main focus of research. In this work, the novel combination of 0D, 1D, and 2D nanomaterials and 3D-nanostructured materials was reported. Specifically, composite materials based on nanoporous activated carbon (AC) and nanowire nickel-doped copper hydroxide compounds (NiDMR) were utilized as the electrode materials, whereas MXenes and carbon dots (CDs) were employed as the diffusion- and surface-process enhancers, respectively. The AC/NiDMR composite electrode yielded an areal capacitance of 71.2 mF cm−2. Upon adding 10 wt% of CDs and MXenes separately, the capacitance was improved by 122.2% and 158.3%, respectively. The highest specific capacitance of 126.9 mF cm−2 was obtained by adding both 5 wt% of CDs and 5 wt% of MXenes, which corresponds to a 178.2% enhancement compared to the pure AC/NiDMR electrode. Extensive analysis shows the synergistically contributing roles of AC, NiDMR, CDs, and MXenes to the enhanced supercapacitor performance. This ground-breaking supercapacitor has the potential to be used in the next-generation energy storage systems based on its improved electrochemical properties, and this novel strategy features opportunities for the unique utilization of nanostructured, multi-component materials in energy storage devices.

head.ce:title: High-performance supercapacitors using synergistic hierarchical Ni-doped copper compounds/activated carbon composites with MXenes and carbon dots as simultaneous performance enhancers

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: For the fabrication of high-performance supercapacitors, materials with nano-array architecture and the use of multiple-component electrodes are emerging as the main focus of research. In this work, the novel combination of 0D, 1D, and 2D nanomaterials and 3D-nanostructured materials was reported. Specifically, composite materials based on nanoporous activated carbon (AC) and nanowire nickel-doped copper hydroxide compounds (NiDMR) were utilized as the electrode materials, whereas MXenes and carbon dots (CDs) were employed as the diffusion- and surface-process enhancers, respectively. The AC/NiDMR composite electrode yielded an areal capacitance of 71.2 mF cm−2. Upon adding 10 wt% of CDs and MXenes separately, the capacitance was improved by 122.2% and 158.3%, respectively. The highest specific capacitance of 126.9 mF cm−2 was obtained by adding both 5 wt% of CDs and 5 wt% of MXenes, which corresponds to a 178.2% enhancement compared to the pure AC/NiDMR electrode. Extensive analysis shows the synergistically contributing roles of AC, NiDMR, CDs, and MXenes to the enhanced supercapacitor performance. This ground-breaking supercapacitor has the potential to be used in the next-generation energy storage systems based on its improved electrochemical properties, and this novel strategy features opportunities for the unique utilization of nanostructured, multi-component materials in energy storage devices.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Both energy use and environmental pollution are receiving a lot of attention as a result of human society's large and fast expansion. Renewable energies, such as wind energy, solar energy, and hydropower, are gaining attention and will become important sources of energy in the future. Supercapacitors have generated a lot of interest in the field of sustainable energy research due to their advantages of high power density, long cycle life, and fast charge/discharge rate [1]. Despite various advantages, supercapacitors suffer mainly from low energy density. Electrode material is a key element in determining the supercapacitor performance because it controls the capacitance, voltage range, and device stability [2]. In an electric double-layer capacitive (EDLC) mechanism, carbon-based materials are frequently employed as the electrode's main components, whereas conducting polymers and metal-based materials are frequently used in pseudocapacitors [3]. Recent advances in straightforward material design and synthesis have opened up new possibilities for the investigation of novel, high-performance electrodes to address the shortcomings of supercapacitors. Many efforts have been made to design and synthesize electrode materials with unique combinations, architectures, and morphologies.

There have been numerous attempts at innovative electrode design to fabricate an electrode with various combinations of materials. It has been shown that the performance of such electrode can be improved by combining the unique properties of each constituent. One of the most commonly used strategies is to combine EDLC and pseudocapacitive materials. EDLC materials provide a large surface area and device stability, but have a low energy density [4]. Pseudocapacitive materials promote the energy density; however, the device frequently sacrifices rate and cycle performance because they store charges via slow redox-based and quasi-irreversible mechanisms [5]. Therefore, combining EDLC and pseudocapacitive materials has the potential to overcome these shortcomings, but the ratio and architecture of the components must be deeply investigated and optimized. Building an integrated structure that properly addresses the structural characteristics and electroactivities of each component ensures effective ion/charge transport and homogeneous interface/chemical distributions at the nanoscale is a fundamental challenge in this area [6]. To date, electrochemical performance for multi-component electrodes has been mostly unsatisfactory due to the lack of well-defined micro-/nano-structures and an understanding of how the materials interact and contribute to the performance. Furthermore, the potential synergistic effects between various constituents have not yet been fully appreciated.

A number of emerging materials for the fabrication of multiple-component electrodes have been exponentially developed in recent years. Copper-based compounds are of great interest for electrochemical applications due to their low cost, low toxicity, and abundant supply in nature [7]. Numerous studies on the creation of copper hydroxide-based electrode materials with a variety of nano-architectures have been published, showing high specific capacitance. P.W. Yuan et al. reported the use of nickel/copper carbonate hydroxide, giving a high specific capacitance of 1017.3 F g−1 and an underappreciated 68.5% retention after only 4,000 cycles [8]. Wang et al. reported a high specific capacitance of 1780 F g−1 from the use of fluorine-doped nickel-copper layered double hydroxide but a relatively low retention of 59% after 10,000 cycles [9]. Although copper hydroxide-based compounds have been reported previously, their use as composite materials, which would simultaneously provide high capacitance and especially good cycling performance, has been rarely investigated.

Another interesting class of transition metal-based materials is based on the 2D layered transition metal carbide/nitride, known as MXenes. Since their discovery in 2011, MXenes have emerged as the promising electrode material candidates for supercapacitors due to outstanding surface hydrophilicity, diverse chemistry, strong mechanical characteristics, and excellent electrical conductivity [10]. Layer-structured materials are particularly intriguing because they can offer a pathway to ion transport for high-efficiency charge storage and discharge [11]. Composites based on MXenes and copper-based compounds for supercapacitors have been reported and showed excellent performance and cycling stability, including CuS (336.7 F g−1) [12], CuCo2S4 (706.5 F g−1) [13], and copper(II) phthalocyanine (786.1 F g−1) [14], but none was reported in conjunction with copper hydroxide-based compounds.

Among several unique and cutting-edge carbon-based nanomaterials studied in recent years, one of them is called carbon dots (CDs). These zero-dimensional carbon nanoparticles are known as promising materials for supercapacitor applications because of their excellent surface wettability, various surface functional groups, high chemical stability, ease of production, nanometer size, electroactive nature, and straightforward synthesis [15]. CDs can increase the conductivity of composite materials and electrode wettability and speed up the surface charge transfer, allowing for more efficient charge storage [16]. Our group and many other groups recently utilized CDs from various sources to improve both specific capacitance and cycling stability of supercapacitors based on activated carbon [17], TiO2 [18], nickel-based metal organic framework [19], NiCo LDHs [20], and also copper-based compounds, including CuO [21], CuS [22], Cu2SnS3, and Cu4SnS4 [23]. However, no report was published on using CDs and copper hydroxide-based compounds.

In this work, a novel design of supercapacitor electrode that includes 0D (carbon dots), 1D (nickel-doped copper hydroxide-based compounds), 2D (MXenes), and 3D (activated carbon) materials was demonstrated (Fig. 1 ). The novel nickel-doped copper hydroxide-based compounds, rouaite and malachite, were investigated for the first time as supercapacitor electrode materials. Malachite and rouaite were simultaneously synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal process. Additionally, they were nickel-doped as the facilely redox-active nickel ions have been reported to enhance supercapacitor performance [24,25]. The activated carbon and carbon dots were simultaneously synthesized from cassava peels. Based on these results, by combining multiple components that each individually contributes to the charge storage, the advantageous properties of electrodes towards supercapacitance performance were demonstrated. Our novel-designed electrodes will be useful for energy storage devices, but not limited to supercapacitors, thanks to their improved electrochemical properties and stability.

Synthesis of AC, CDs, and MXenes, preparation of electrolyte, material characterizations, electrochemical measurements, and calculation of specific capacitance, energy density, and power density are provided in the supporting information.

All chemicals were purchased from Merck and were of analytical grade. A cellulose dialysis membrane (MWCO = 1 kDa) was purchased from Spectrum Labs. Cassava peels and cassava starch were obtained from local plantations in Thailand. Carbon paper (resistivity <3 m
Ω
cm−2) was used as a current collector. Deionized (DI) water was used in all experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Nickel-doped copper hydroxide compounds
ce:section.ce:para: Nickel-doped malachite/rouaite (NiDMR) was synthesized via a hydrothermal method. 0.1 M Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, 0.1 M Cu(NO3)2·6H2O, and 0.2 M urea (CH4N2O) were mixed in DI water (60 mL) with vigorous agitation for 15 min. The homogeneous mixture was then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 120°C for 12 h. After the reaction was completed, it was cooled down to room temperature, filtered through a filter paper, rinsed with DI water and ethanol several times, and dried at 60°C overnight to obtain NiDMR as a blue-green powder.

AC and NiDMR were mixed in the different weight ratio of 3:1, 1:1, and 1:3%wt in DI water (10 mL), which are denoted as Composite1, Composite2, and Composite3, respectively (Table 1 ). After that, the mixture was ultrasonicated for 1 h and heated in an oven at 80°C for 24 h.

AC, NiDMR, composites were used as active materials. They were mixed with carbon black as a conducting agent and poly(vinylidene fluoride) as a binder in a ratio of 80:15:5%wt in the presence of N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone as a solvent. The homogeneous mixture was coated on carbon paper as a current collector and dried at 80°C overnight. The coated area of the electrode material is 1 cm2 and the mass loading is about 3-10 mg. To study the effects of additives on supercapacitor performance, CDs or MXenes were mixed with the AC/NiDMR electrode materials at 5, 10, and 15%wt. Table 2 designates the amount of electrode materials in weight percentage. To study the resistance and stability, each system was fabricated in a Swagelok cell with an electrode diameter of 1 cm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of AC, NiDMR, and AC/NiDMR composites
ce:section.ce:para: The AC was prepared from cassava peel following our previously reported method using thermal activation and potassium hydroxide as an activating agent in a weight ratio of 1:3 carbon char to KOH at a temperature of 750°C for 1 h, the synthesis procedures were described in the Supplement Materials [17]. The nitrogen absorption/desorption profile of the resulting AC shows a type-IV isotherm (Fig. S2a), validating the presence of micropores and mesopores in the framework of AC [26]. The specific surface area, pore volume, and average pore size are 903 m2 g−1, 0.64 cm3 g−1, and 2.56 nm, respectively (Table S1). The relatively large surface area and mesopores suggest the suitability of the AC for the EDLC mechanism. The SEM technique was used to investigate the morphological properties of the AC. As shown in Fig. 2 a and b, the SEM images show a typical appearance of biomass-derived, KOH-etched AC with a rough surface and irregularities. In addition, SEM-EDS was used to investigate the elemental components of the AC. As shown in Fig. S3, the AC was mainly composed of carbon (20.2%) and oxygen (79.8%) and had well-distributed constituent elements in the framework. Therefore, from both nitrogen absorption/desorption profile and SEM-EDS analysis, the obtained AC is an excellent supercapacitor electrode material for charge storage via the EDLC mechanism.

The nickel-doped malachite/rouaite (NiDMR) was synthesized via a hydrothermal method using copper(II) nitrate, nickel(II) nitrate, and urea as starting materials. Recently, a hydrothermal technique in aqueous systems has grown in popularity as an easy and effective way to create ordered nanoarray structures [27]. To grow nanostructured materials, this technique depends on the chemical reactions and solubility changes of constituents in a sealed, heated aqueous solution above ambient temperature and pressure [28]. In this regard, the one-step, self-templated growth of nickel-doped malachite/rouaite mixture using the hydrothermal method was demonstrated. Under thermal treatment, urea is converted to ammonia and carbon dioxide, which further form ammonium ion, hydroxide ion, and carbonate ion Eq. 1-(4). Subsequently, copper(II) ion, carbonate ion, and hydroxide ion form the malachite (Cu2(CO3)(OH)2) (Eq. 5), whereas copper(II) ion, nitrate ion, and hydroxide ion formed rouaite (Cu2(NO3)(OH)3) (Eq. 6).

NiDMR was first characterized using XRD. The XRD pattern of NiDMR (Fig. 3 a) shows a combination of primary signals of malachite at 14.7o, 17.5o, 24.0o, and 31.3o (PDF file #00-041-1390) and rouaite at 12.7o, 25.7o, 33.6o, and 35.5o (PDF file #01-075-5127), which can be deconvoluted to the contents of malachite (60%) and rouaite (40%). Because the nickel-based compound was not found in the XRD pattern, nickel thus acted as the dopant to the malachite/rouaite composite. Because the Ni2+ ion size is 72 pm, which is close to that of Cu2+ (73 pm), the Ni2+ ion can replace the Cu2+ ion or sit in the free volume of the crystalline structure [29]. Despite their similar sizes, Cu2+ has a much higher binding/chelating ability than Ni2+ [30,31], and therefore, the copper hydroxide compounds were more kinetically and thermodynamically favored, while nickel ions acted as dopants. The SEM images of NiDMR exhibit a hierarchical structure with nanowires stemming from the surface. The width and length of the nanowires are approximately 35 and 300 nm, respectively (Fig. 2c and d). The formation of the nanowire structure is believed to arise from the vertical self-growth mechanism of copper hydroxide compounds, assisted by the high pressure and temperature of the hydrothermal method [32]. The oriented arrays of nanowires would provide a large active surface and deep penetration of electrolytic ions for efficient charge storage. Based on the SEM-EDS images, C (10.7%), O (38.7%), Cu (16.2%), and Ni (34.4%) are well distributed (Fig. S4). The high-resolution XPS spectra exhibit a weak nitrogen signal and the predominant Ni2+ and Cu2+ peaks (Fig. S5). Similar to the previous report [33], it is worth pointing out that nitrogen was not detected using the EDS technique because (1) the nitrogen atomic concentration is only 2.3%, (2) the nitrate ions are located within the inner layers of the crystal structure, and (3) nitrogen produces a weak response in EDS [34] (Fig. 3c and d). The functional groups of NiDMR were examined using FT-IR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. S6a, the NiDMR spectrum shows the hydroxyl group, carbonate group, and nitrate group at 3328-3408, 1380-1460, and 1360-1380 cm−1, respectively [35,36]. The surface area of NiDMR was determined to be 151 m2 g−1 (Table S1), which is relatively high for inorganic hydroxides [8,37,38]. This is due to its nanowire structure in the nanometer length scale. Because NiDMR has a high surface area and contains Cu and Ni transition metals, which are well-known redox active elements [39,40], it is a promising candidate as a pseudocapacitive material.

As described in the introduction section, to boost the specific capacitance by integrating EDLC and pseudocapacitive materials, AC and NiDMR were combined to obtain the AC/NiDMR composites. Three composites (Composite1-3) were prepared using different relative amounts of AC and NiDMR. As a representative, Composite2 at a 1:1 weight ratio shows a rough surface, contributed by the morphological features of its parent AC and NiDMR (Fig. 2e). As shown in Fig. 2f-i, the SEM-EDS mapping images of the AC/NiDMR composite display the presence of well-distributed carbon, oxygen, copper, and nickel, suggesting that AC and NiDMR were well mixed in the composite. The FT-IR spectrum of Composite2 shows the combined signals of AC and NiDMR (Fig. S6b). The specific surface area of the composite was measured to be 665 m2 g−1, which is lower than the original AC but higher than the NiDMR (Fig. S2a and b). This is due to the infiltrated phenomenon between the two materials and also suggests homogeneity and compatibility between them.

The XRD patterns of the composites were obtained and compared with those of AC and NiDMR (Fig. 3a). The XRD spectrum of AC exhibits a broad, hill-like diffraction signal at 24o, corresponding to the (002) plane of the amorphous carbon. The XRD patterns of all composites show sharp peaks of NiDMR and a broad signal of AC, demonstrating the presence of both AC and NiDMR in the composites. As shown in Fig. 3b, the Raman spectrum of AC exhibits two peaks at 1320 and 1578 cm−1, which correspond to the D band (disordered carbon) and the G band (graphitic carbon), respectively. The D band is more dominant than the G band, indicating that the AC is highly amorphous, which is typical for biomass-derived ACs [41,42]. The primary Raman peak of NiDMR was observed at 1056 cm−1, which corresponded to the signals of the carbonate/nitrate groups, also confirming the presence of malachite and rouaite [43,44]. Meanwhile, the Raman spectra of all AC/NiDMR composites reveal a combination of peaks from AC and NiDMR, demonstrating the presence of these two materials in the composites. Furthermore, the ID/IG ratios of AC, Composite1, Composite2, and Composite3 are 1.21, 1.15, 1.12, and 1.09, respectively. It means that increasing the NiDMR/AC ratio decreased the ID/IG values, which is an enhancement of crystallinity due to the NiDMR. Both Raman and XRD results therefore prove that the AC/NiDMR composites contained both AC and NiDMR.

The AC, NiDMR, and AC/NiDMR composites were first tested as supercapacitor electrode materials. The electrochemical properties of the fabricated electrodes were measured using a quasi-solid gel electrolyte made from cassava starch with a potential window between -0.25 and 0.25 V. The cassava starch was chosen as the biorenewable polymer host because it offers high stability while maintaining a high specific capacitance [45]. As shown in Fig. 4 a, cyclic voltammograms (CVs) of all electrodes at a scan rate of 30 mV s−1 are demonstrated. The AC and NiDMR electrodes gave 42.7 and 16.5 mF cm−2, respectively. All AC/NiDMR composite electrodes possessed larger CV curves, i.e., higher specific capacitances, than the AC and NiDMR electrodes. This means that a combination of AC and NiDMR yielded the beneficially integrated effects contributing to the charge storage and a combination of EDLC and pseudocapacitive materials gave a better capacitance than each material alone. The ratio between AC and NiDMR also played a key role in the supercapacitor performance. It is clear that the Composite2 electrode (71.2 mF cm−2) gave a higher specific capacitance than the Composite1 (60.5 mF cm−2) and Composite3 (49.1 mF cm−2) electrodes. This means that a ratio of 1:1 between AC and NiDMR is optimum for achieving the combined effects of EDLC and pseudocapacitor, as compared to the 3:1 and 1:3 ratios. The GCD profiles of all electrodes are presented in Fig. 4b, and all samples produced symmetric charge-discharge curves and negligible internal resistances (IR drop), indicating that the electrodes have good charge transfer and low resistance. Based on the specific capacitance, it can be concluded that the Composite2 electrode is the promising candidate for the fabrication of a practical supercapacitor. The series resistance of all electrodes was further analyzed using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). As shown in Fig. 4c, the Nyquist plot of the Composite2 electrode shows the lowest series resistance (3.8
Ω
), indicating the consistent correlation with its highest specific capacitance. The series resistances of Composite1, Composite3, AC, and NiDMR are 4.2, 4.4, 5.0, and 5.4
Ω
, respectively, which also correlate well with their specific capacitances. This indicates that the specific capacitance is controlled by the series resistance, which is in turn governed by the electrode constituents and their ratio. The rate performance of all electrodes was calculated from the CV profiles measured at a scan rate from 6 to 100 mV s−1 (Fig. S7a-e). A plot of specific capacitance and scan rate is depicted in Fig. 4d. The highest rate performance of 95% was obtained from the AC electrode, which is a well-known advantage of fast-response EDLC. Meanwhile, the NiDMR electrode provided only 51% rate performance, which is due to the sluggishly diffusion-controlled characteristics of the metal oxide-based pseudocapacitors. However, the rate performances of the Composite1 (81%), Composite2 (85%), and Composite3 (65%) electrodes were improved when compared to that of the pure NiDMR. Composite3 has the lowest rate performance because of the highest amount of NiDMR in the electrode. Although Composite2 had a greater amount of NiDMR than Composite1, it gave a better rate performance than Composite1, indicating the positive effects provided by the optimum amounts of AC and NiDMR.

To better understand the positive effects occurring on the composite electrodes, the electrochemical surface area (ECSA) was calculated from the CV profiles obtained using different scan rates from 6 to 100 mV s−1. The current densities against scan rates were plotted (Fig. S7f). The slope of the curve is the Cdl value. The high Cdl is equivalent to the high ECSA and a large number of active sites for charge storage [46]. The Composite2 electrode had the highest Cdl of 49.8 mF cm−2, followed by Composite1 (31.2 mF cm−2), Composite3 (18.5 mF cm−2), AC (17.2 mF cm−2), and NiDMR (3.5 mF cm−2), agreeing well with the ranking of their specific capacitances and series resistances. This result verifies that Composite2 has the most electroactive sites, contributing to the highest specific capacitance and lowest series resistance as compared to the parent AC, NiDMR, and other composite ratios.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of CDs and MXenes on electrochemical performance
ce:section.ce:para: To boost the specific capacitance of our supercapacitor, CDs and MXenes were added to the Composite2 electrode and the electrochemical behaviors were studied. First, the CDs were mixed with Composite2 at three weight ratios of 5, 10, and 15%wt. The Composite2 electrode containing 5%wt CDs gave a specific capacitance of 75.1 mF cm−2, which is slightly higher than the pristine Composite2 electrode and equivalent to a 105.5% increase (Fig. 5 a). When increasing the amount of the CDs to 10%wt, a further improved specific capacitance of 87 mF cm−2 was obtained, which corresponds to the 122.2% improvement. However, when the CDs were further raised to 15%wt, the specific capacitance dropped to 78.7 mF cm−2. We believe that adding 15%wt CDs can have deteriorating effects on the supercapacitor performance via an increase in electrical resistance and the dissolution of electrode materials into the electrolyte due to the high aqueous solubility of the CDs. Nonetheless, the Composite2/CDs to 10%wt electrode is still better than the original Composite2 electrode. The effects of the CDs will be further examined and discussed later in this paper. The symmetric GCD curves agree with the CV results, in which Composite2 gave the longest charge and discharge times, corresponding to the highest specific capacitance (Fig. 5b).

In addition to the CDs, the effects of MXenes as additives on the electrochemical behaviors were investigated. MXenes were added to Composite2 at three different weight ratios of 5, 10, and 15%wt. As shown in Fig. 5c, the Composite2/10%wt MXenes electrode had the highest specific capacitance of 112.7 mF cm−2, a 158.3% improvement over the original Composite2 electrode. On the other hand, the 5%wt and 15%wt MXenes-containing Composite2 electrodes gave a specific capacitance of 100.3 and 89.3 mF cm−2, respectively, which are lower than the 10%wt/Composite2 electrode, but all are still better than the pristine Composite2 electrode. This means the addition of MXenes helped raise the specific capacitance of this composite, and 10%wt MXenes were optimum. The GCD profiles of Composite2/MXenes electrodes correspond to the CV results (Fig. 5d). This emphasizes the favorable effects facilitated by the MXenes in the Composite2 electrode.

Because the Composite2 electrode with either 10%wt CDs or 10%wt MXenes gave a considerable improvement in the specific capacitance, in this experiment, 5%wt CDs and 5%wt MXenes were added together to the Composite2 electrode. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the CV profile of the Composite2 electrode with 5%wt CDs and 5%wt MXenes displays a curve with a quasi-rectangular shape and a high-current response. At a scan rate of 30 mV s−1, a specific capacitance of 126.9 mF cm−2 was obtained, which corresponds to a 178.2% enhancement when compared to the Composite2 electrode. Interestingly, it was also higher than the Composite2 electrode with either 10%wt CDs or 10%wt MXenes. This indicates the synergistically contributing effects of both CDs and MXenes when used simultaneously. The GCD profile of the CDs/MXenes/Composite2 electrode (Fig. 6b) demonstrates a combination of shapes; (1) a quasi-triangular shape at high potentials, which is the result of the CDs, and (2) a low-slope shape at low potentials, which is the effect of MXenes. The Nyquist plots of the composite electrodes were obtained (Fig. 6c). An inset showed the equivalent electrical circuit used for EIS fitting. Rs and Rct are the combined series and charge-transfer resistances, respectively. W is the Warburg element. CPEEDL and CPEP are the constant phase elements representing an electrical double-layer capacitor and a pseudocapacitor, respectively. Adding 10%wt CDs to the Composite2 surprisingly increased the series resistance from 4.2 to 5.1
Ω
while adding 10%wt MXenes increased the resistance to 7.4
Ω
. However, adding 5%wt CDs and 5%wt MXenes had a series resistance of 6.0
Ω
. The Bode plots (Fig. 6d) displayed the phase angles of Composite2, Composite2/10%wt CDs, Composite2/10%wt MXenes, and Composite2/5%wt CDs/5%wt MXenes at y-intercepts of -83.2°, -79.8°, -80.4°, and -80.9°, respectively. The phase angles close to -90° indicated that all fabricated supercapacitors behaved like excellent supercapacitors [18]. These results correspond with the Nyquist plots, in which the low series resistances were observed. Despite having a higher series resistance than the Composite2 electrode, the simultaneous addition of both CDs and MXenes to the Composite2 electrode significantly elevated the specific capacitance and suggests that, in addition to series resistance, there are other factors contributing to the supercapacitor performance, possibly including electrode/electrolyte interactions, ion adsorption/desorption/diffusion, material compatibility, and surface properties, and these factors must be optimized to achieve the best supercapacitor performance.

Our supercapacitors were next investigated for cycling stability and temperature effects for practicality tests. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the capacitance retention of the NiDMR electrode dramatically decreased to 75% after 500 cycles and then further dropped to 52% and 46% after 10,000 and 20,000 cycles, respectively, which is the well-known drawback of the pseudocapacitor. The AC electrode exhibited much better retention than the NiDMR electrode, giving 91% retention at 10,000 cycles and 86% at 20,000 cycles, which is a typical characteristic of EDLC materials. Although the Composite2 electrode contained AC and NiDMR at a 1:1 ratio, it provided a comparable capacitance retention of 86% and 83% after 10,000 and 20,000 cycles, respectively, to the pure AC electrode. This strengthens the beneficially combined effects of AC and NiDMR as well as their homogeneously infiltrated structures that slowed down electrode degradation. When 10%wt CDs were added to the Composite2 electrode, they improved not only the specific capacitance but also the capacitance retention to 89% and 86% after 10,000 and 20,000 cycles, respectively, while the 10%wt MXenes-decorated Composite2 electrode had a very slightly inferior capacitance retention of 88% after 10,000 cycles and 81% after 20,000 cycles. Interestingly, the Composite2/5%wt CDs/5%wt MXenes electrode provided a capacitance retention of 90% after 10,000 cycles and 86% after 20,000 cycles, which are comparable to the AC electrode and better than the Composite2 electrode with the addition of either CDs or MXenes. This also emphasizes the positive effects of the simultaneous use of both CDs and MXenes as additives, which improved not only the specific capacitance but also the cycling stability. After 100,000 cycles, the capacitance was still retained at 76% (Fig. 7b), which is considered excellent among metal oxide-based supercapacitors in the literature [47,48]. Fig. 7c shows the GCD profiles at the 1st and 100,000th cycles. The supercapacitor was still able to maintain a high coulombic efficiency. For real applications, a wide temperature range for the use of the supercapacitor is important. The fabricated supercapacitor was measured for the specific capacitance at a temperature from -40 to 60°C. As shown in Fig. 7d and e, at an extremely cold condition (-40°C), the relative specific capacitance dropped only by 19% because the viscosity of an electrolyte increased whereas the ionic conductivity reduced [49]. On the other hand, at 60°C the specific capacitance rose to 139% because of a decrease in viscosity and an increase in ionic conductivity. In this work, our supercapacitors demonstrated excellent cycling stability, a wide temperature range, and the potential for a variety of applications. Compared to previous reports on composite electrodes based on carbon-based materials/copper-based compounds, our supercapacitor performance was the best (Table S2). This is due to the combination of all four materials in the electrode that synergistically contributed to the extraordinary performance.

The Dunn's method was used to assess the capacitive behaviors further. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the AC and NiDMR electrodes displayed the predominant surface and diffusion contributions, respectively, while the Composite2 electrode exhibited a combination of both, consistent with the Cottrell's analysis. When the CDs were added to the Composite2 electrode, the surface-controlled process was improved. On the other hand, when the MXenes were added to the Composite2 electrode, the diffusion-controlled process was enhanced. These results confirm their roles as surface- and diffusion-process enhancers, respectively, which is again consistent with the Cottrell's analysis. Because the Composite2 electrode with 5%wt CDs and 5%wt MXenes provided the highest specific capacitance, the optimal adsorption and diffusion contributions were obtained in this electrode system. Percentages of surface and diffusion contributions in the CV profiles are provided in Fig. 8b-g. From these Cottrell's and Dunn's analyses, it can be concluded that: (1) CDs and MXenes acted as surface and diffusion enhancers, respectively and using them simultaneously yielded better results than using each one separately. (2) Both surface- and diffusion-controlled processes are critical to supercapacitor, and an optimal balance between these two processes must be achieved to maximize the performance.

To further confirm that the CDs and MXenes acted as surface- and diffusion-process enhancers, respectively, we fabricated three types of electrodes: AC, AC/10%wt CDs, and AC/10%wt MXenes. As shown in Fig. 9 a, it was found that the AC/10%wt MXenes electrode gave a higher specific capacitance (72.0 mF cm−2) compared to the AC electrode (42.7 mF cm−2), but adding 10%wt CDs to the AC electrode negligibly improved the capacitance (47.8 mF cm−2). This demonstrates that to enhance the performance of EDLC materials, diffusion-process enhancers must be added to the electrode. Additionally, three electrodes based on NiDMR, including NiDMR, NiDMR/10%wt CDs, and NiDMR/10%wt MXenes, were fabricated (Fig. 9b). It was found that the NiDMR/10%wt CDs electrode gave a higher specific capacitance (71.2 mF cm−2) compared to the NiDMR electrode (16.5 mF cm−2) and the NiDMR/10%wt MXenes electrode (48.7 mF cm−2). From these results, it can be concluded that to enhance the specific capacitance of pseudocapacitors, surface-process enhancers should be utilized. These experiments strengthen the fact that combining materials with contributing roles and selecting materials with synergistic contributions are critical to the supercapacitor performance.

From the above studies, the CDs and MXenes had profound effects on the surface and diffusion processes and supercapacitor performance. To investigate the influence of these additives on the electrode/electrolyte interface, the contact angles of KOH solution droplets on the electrode surfaces were measured (Fig. 10 ). A contact angle of 101o was obtained from the Composite2 electrode, while Composite2/10%wt CDs, Composite2/10%wt MXenes, and Composite2/5%wt CDs/5%wt MXenes exhibited the contact angles of 93, 93, and 84o, respectively. The Composite2 electrode is thus the most hydrophobic, and adding the CDs or MXenes increased the hydrophilicity of the electrodes. This is due to the presence of hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of CDs and MXenes. Surprisingly, adding 5%wt CDs and 5%wt MXenes together yielded the most hydrophilic surface. The contact angle-specific capacitance relationship indicates the strong correlation between supercapacitor performance and electrode hydrophilicity. After ten seconds, the contact angles of all electrodes dropped due to the absorption of KOH droplet into the electrode materials. Especially for the CDs-containing electrodes, the contact angles drastically reduced. This result confirms that the CDs significantly improved the surface wettability. Interestingly, at twenty seconds, the KOH droplet was completely absorbed into the Composite2/5%wt CDs/5%wt MXenes electrode while those on the other electrodes were still visible. Although CDs are more hydrophilic than MXenes, a combination of the two produced a more hydrophilic surface than when each one was used separately, suggesting some synergistic influences from both CDs and MXenes on the hydrophilicity. This contact angle measurement confirms that the Composite2/5%wt CDs/5%wt MXenes electrode has the most hydrophilic surface, which is favorable for electrolyte penetration into inner materials and resulted in the highest specific capacitance among all electrodes. This finding also confirms the statement that there are several factors contributing to the supercapacitor performance, especially electrode/electrolyte interactions, ion adsorption/diffusion, and surface properties.

Based on the results and analysis above, the AC and NiDMR stored charges via EDLC and pseudocapacitive mechanisms, respectively, and the CDs and MXenes acted as surface- and diffusion-process enhancers. AC is the EDLC material, while malachite and rouaite in NiDMR are known to undergo redox reactions to supply electrons, Eq. 7-(9), which were responsible for the pseudocapacitive performance of our fabricated supercapacitors [51].

As confirmed by the well-distributed elements and homogeneity shown in the SEM-EDS images, the 3D-structured AC and 1D-structured NiDMR in the composite could provide a good contact, infiltration, and bridging for charge/ion storage [52]. Furthermore, the hydrophilic features of NiDMR, which are brought about by the carbonate and nitrate ions, can increase the likelihood of more favorable interactions between electrode materials and at the electrode/electrolyte interface [53]. Therefore, the AC/NiDMR composites yielded a better performance than the pristine AC or NiDMR. As additives, the 0D and nanometer size of the CDs allowed them to penetrate into the gaps of the AC/NiDMR composite and provided even better electrical contact, continuous pathways, and material adhesion [15–17]. As confirmed by the contact angle measurement, the CDs improved the surface wettability of the electrode, producing better ion adsorption and interfacial interactions, resulting in enhanced surface processes as verified by the electrochemical analysis. Meanwhile, the 2D MXenes are well known for facilitating ion intercalation and providing pseudocapacitance, in which they were indeed found to improve the diffusion processes [11,54,55]. As proved by the contact angle measurement, the terminal groups of MXenes make MXenes hydrophilic, which can partly contribute to ion adsorption and diffusion in the inner materials [56]. In addition to the significant enhancement in specific capacitance, we believe that these four components with distinct sizes, dimensions, and properties could help reduce material aggregation and enhance compatibility and favorable contact among them. This would in turn improve the reversibility of charge storage processes and suppress the deterioration of the electrodes, leading to the enhanced cycling stability. In essence, based on several positive contributing factors described above, the greatly enhanced specific capacitance and cycling stability of the supercapacitors using a novel combination of composite materials were achieved in this work.

To conclude, this work demonstrates a novel combination of 0D, 1D, and 2D nanomaterials and 3D-nanostructured materials for the fabrication of a composite supercapacitor electrode. A ratio of 1:1 between AC and NiDMR was found to yield the highest areal capacitance among all the ratios tested. Adding the CDs and MXenes together, the capacitance was ultimately improved by 178.2% because they enhanced the surface and diffusion processes, respectively, that are necessary for the highly efficient charge storage. The cycling stability was also significantly improved with the addition of both CDs and MXenes. Extensive analysis suggests that a combination of unique morphologies and properties of all four materials and their contributing effects are crucial to the enhanced performance. As a result, we have demonstrated a novel strategy and fundamental knowledge of utilizing materials with synergistic properties. Possible future work can be performed to further improve the performance, including the investigation of other reaction conditions to obtain different NiDMR compositions and the development of other copper hydroxide compounds. According to these findings, the multi-component composite electrodes are a viable option for high-efficiency supercapacitors, and the application of our developed strategy to other energy storage devices will be considerably beneficial.
The raw data required to reproduce these findings will be made available on request.
